case_name: Intel Corporation vs S.P. Gupta And Others
court: High Court of Delhi at New Delhi
facts: Intel Corporation (Plaintiff), a multinational owner of the well-known mark 'INTEL', filed suit in 2006 against three Indian entities and their director S.P. Gupta for trademark infringement and passing off, alleging use of the mark 'INTEL' in corporate names and the domain www.intelgasgadgets.com for LPG regulators and related products. Plaintiff asserted registrations for 'INTEL' in Classes 9 and 18 and reliance on global goodwill and prior recognition as a well-known mark. Defendants continued business under names incorporating 'INTEL' despite cease-and-desist letters; proceedings ran through evidence and mediation, and one defendant's principal, S.P. Gupta, died during the litigation. By the hearing, defendants' representative stated the entities were not conducting business and agreed not to use the mark "INTEL" as long as damages/costs were not pressed.
arguments_claimant: The Plaintiff argued that it is the registered proprietor of the mark 'INTEL' (registrations in Classes 9 & 18) and that the mark has been declared a well-known trademark under Section 2(zg) of the Trade Marks Act, 1999, supporting entitlement to injunction for trademark infringement and passing off. Plaintiff relied on prior decisions recognizing the well-known status of 'INTEL' and evidence of extensive use, advertising expenditure, and global goodwill.
arguments_respondent: Defendants filed written statements raising issues including delay/laches and mis-joinder; they did not obtain an interim injunction during the suit. At final hearing defendants' counsel informed the Court that the companies were not conducting business and, through legal heirs, undertook not to use the mark 'INTEL' so long as the Plaintiff did not press for damages or costs.
ratio_decidendi: The Court's decision rested on the factual stance that "the lis no longer exists between the parties" and that the Defendants "are willing to suffer permanent injunction in terms of the prayer clause 37(i) & (ii) in the plaint." Applying those facts, the Court held: "Under these circumstances, the suit of the Plaintiff is decreed in terms of the paragraph 37 (i) & (ii) of the plaint." The principle applied is that where the defendant concedes and undertakes not to continue the impugned use (and the dispute is therefore effectively extinguished), a permanent injunction in the terms sought may be granted and other reliefs need not be pressed or awarded. The Court also noted the Plaintiff's reliance on the mark's well-known status but based the operative decree on the defendants' willingness to suffer the injunction.
final_order: The suit is decreed in terms of paragraph 37(i) & (ii) of the plaint granting a permanent injunction restraining the Defendants from using the name/mark 'INTEL' and related domain/email use; all other reliefs were not pressed. Defendant No.2 shall effect change of company name with the Registrar of Companies within three months; the suit and pending applications are disposed of.
summary: Intel Corporation sued three Indian companies and their director for using the mark 'INTEL' in company names and the domain intelgasgadgets.com, alleging trademark infringement and passing off. During final hearing defendants represented they were not conducting business and undertook not to use the mark; the Court found the lis no longer existed and granted the permanent injunction sought by Intel. The Court did not grant damages or other reliefs which were not pressed, and ordered one defendant company to change its name within three months. The suit was decreed in the limited terms pleaded for injunctive relief.
embedding_summary: Intel Corporation v. S.P. Gupta And Others, High Court of Delhi, CS (COMM) 179/2018, decision 7 October 2022. Intel (Plaintiff) sued Indian entities Intel Gas Gadgets Pvt. Ltd., Intel Gas Guard Pvt. Ltd., Intel Safety Devices and director S.P. Gupta for trademark infringement and passing off for use of 'INTEL' in company names and domain www.intelgasgadgets.com relating to LPG regulators/valves. Plaintiff relied on registrations in Classes 9 & 18 (nos. 678000 and 768180), claimed the mark was a well-known mark under Section 2(zg) Trade Marks Act 1999, and cited prior Delhi decisions Intel v. A. Sailesh (2007 (34) PTC 492) and Intel v. Dinakaran Nair (2006 (33) PTC 345 Del.). Defendants later informed the Court they were not conducting business and undertook not to use the mark; the Court held "the lis no longer exists" and decreed the suit for permanent injunction (paragraph 37(i)&(ii)), declined other reliefs not pressed, and directed change of company name within three months. Outcome: permanent injunction granted; no damages awarded in this judgment.
legal_principles: Consent/Willingness of defendant to 'suffer permanent injunction' can justify granting the permanent injunction where the lis no longer exists
Recognition of a mark as 'well-known' under Section 2(zg) Trade Marks Act 1999 supports entitlement to injunctive relief against infringers
Court may limit decree to reliefs actually pressed by the plaintiff and decline other reliefs if not pressed
Corporate compliance (Registrar of Companies) may be directed as part of injunctive relief (change of company name within fixed period)
cases_cited: Intel Corporation v. A. Sailesh, 2007 (34) PTC 492
Intel Corporation v. Dinakaran Nair & Ors., 2006 (33) PTC 345 Del.

Gupta And Others High Court of Delhi at New Delhi 2022 Intel Corporation (Plaintiff), a multinational owner of the well-known mark 'INTEL', filed suit in 2006 against three Indian entities and their director S.P. Gupta for trademark infringement and passing off, alleging use of the mark 'INTEL' in corporate names and the domain www.intelgasgadgets.com for LPG regulators and related products. Plaintiff asserted registrations for 'INTEL' in Classes 9 and 18 and reliance on global goodwill and prior recognition as a well-known mark. Defendants continued business under names incorporating 'INTEL' despite cease-and-desist letters; proceedings ran through evidence and mediation, and one defendant's principal, S.P. Gupta, died during the litigation. By the hearing, defendants' representative stated the entities were not conducting business and agreed not to use the mark "INTEL" as long as damages/costs were not pressed. The Plaintiff argued that it is the registered proprietor of the mark 'INTEL' (registrations in Classes 9 & 18) and that the mark has been declared a well-known trademark under Section 2(zg) of the Trade Marks Act, 1999, supporting entitlement to injunction for trademark infringement and passing off. Plaintiff relied on prior decisions recognizing the well-known status of 'INTEL' and evidence of extensive use, advertising expenditure, and global goodwill. Defendants filed written statements raising issues including delay/laches and mis-joinder; they did not obtain an interim injunction during the suit. At final hearing defendants' counsel informed the Court that the companies were not conducting business and, through legal heirs, undertook not to use the mark 'INTEL' so long as the Plaintiff did not press for damages or costs. The Court's decision rested on the factual stance that "the lis no longer exists between the parties" and that the Defendants "are willing to suffer permanent injunction in terms of the prayer clause 37(i) & (ii) in the plaint." Applying those facts, the Court held: "Under these circumstances, the suit of the Plaintiff is decreed in terms of the paragraph 37 (i) & (ii) of the plaint." The principle applied is that where the defendant concedes and undertakes not to continue the impugned use (and the dispute is therefore effectively extinguished), a permanent injunction in the terms sought may be granted and other reliefs need not be pressed or awarded. The Court also noted the Plaintiff's reliance on the mark's well-known status but based the operative decree on the defendants' willingness to suffer the injunction. The suit is decreed in terms of paragraph 37(i) & (ii) of the plaint granting a permanent injunction restraining the Defendants from using the name/mark 'INTEL' and related domain/email use; all other reliefs were not pressed. Defendant No.2 shall effect change of company name with the Registrar of Companies within three months; the suit and pending applications are disposed of.
